label: The Moral Argument
text: The Moral Argument for the existence of God

children.label: P1 — If God does not exist, objective moral values do not exist
children.text: P1 — Without God, moral values and duties are merely the products of socio-biological evolution or cultural conditioning — they would be subjective, relative, or illusory rather than genuinely binding and objective.

children.label: Naturalism reduces morality to evolved preferences
children.text: On a purely naturalistic view, moral intuitions are adaptations that promoted reproductive fitness. They carry no more objective authority than a preference for sweet foods — useful but not genuinely obligatory.

children.label: Objective morality can be grounded without God (non-theistic realism)
children.text: Moral realists argue that moral facts are necessary truths, like mathematical facts, that exist independently of any mind — divine or human.

children.label: Abstract moral facts can’t ground moral obligations
children.text: Even if abstract moral truths exist, they cannot by themselves explain why anyone is obligated to act on them. Moral obligation requires a personal authority — a law-giver. Abstract platonic objects make no demands.

children.label: P2 — Objective moral values and duties do exist
children.text: P2 — We know with confidence that certain things are objectively morally wrong (torturing children for fun, genocide, rape) and others objectively good (love, justice, mercy) — independent of personal or cultural opinion.

children.label: Moral intuitions are strong evidence for objective moral facts
children.text: The near-universal human intuition that gratuitous cruelty is wrong is powerful evidence for objective moral facts. Denying this requires a deeper skepticism that undermines all knowledge, including scientific knowledge.

children.label: Moral beliefs vary across cultures — there are no universal facts
children.text: Anthropology shows significant cross-cultural moral disagreement. This suggests moral ‘facts’ are social constructs rather than objective truths.

children.label: Disagreement proves opinion, not the absence of fact
children.text: Historical disagreement about scientific facts did not make science subjective. Moral disagreement is consistent with there being objective moral facts that cultures approximate imperfectly. Moreover, there is broad cross-cultural agreement on core moral norms.

children.label: ∴ God exists as the ground of objective moral reality
children.text: ∴ Since objective moral values and duties exist, and they require a personal ground to be genuinely binding, the best explanation is a morally perfect, personal being — God — whose nature is the standard of goodness and whose commands constitute moral duty.

children.label: God’s nature — not arbitrary will — is the moral standard
children.text: The moral argument does not imply God arbitrarily invents morality (the Euthyphro dilemma). Rather, God’s perfectly good nature is the standard; his commands flow necessarily from that nature.

children.label: Euthyphro: is something good because God wills it, or does God will it because it’s good?
children.text: Plato’s dilemma: if God wills good because it is good, goodness is independent of God. If something is good because God wills it, morality is arbitrary — God could make torture good by decree.

children.label: Third option: God’s nature is the standard; commands express that nature
children.text: The dilemma presents a false dichotomy. God neither conforms to an external standard nor arbitrarily invents one — his essentially good nature is the ultimate standard, and his commands necessarily express it.
